a traditional dance performed by men, (especially) the Aérégbé festival in honor of several deities such as Ọlụ́a. Men often dress in only skirts while they sing derogatory songs used to meant to criticize authoritative chiefs and evildoers.

six pence (sixpence); in modern usage, five kobo

the place of origin for all humans where souls (orí) are created, humans return to their individual ọ̀run after death. It does not represent a specific entity or location, every human has their own origin place.

python; (in particular) African rock python (Python sebae) and the Ball python

a form of divination consisting of the casting of sixteen cowries (ẹyọ). It is primarily by all priests of the orisha; with the exception of babalawos (Ifá priests to divinate to their respective orishas (ex. Èṣù, Ọbàtálá), however, the tool can still be used to consult Ifá. It also consists of a different order of the Odù (in comparison to the Odù Ifá). (For example, the first Odù from the ẹ̀rìndínlógún is Ọ̀kànràn, as opposed to the Ejì Ogbè in the Odù Ifá)
abbreviation of àti bẹ́ẹ̀ bẹ́ẹ̀ lọ (“et cetera; and so on”)
Used after a noun to signify "What about...?"
Used after a noun to signify "What about...?"

bitter tomato, Solanum aethiopicum; garden egg, eggplant
cab; taxi (in particular) a private car operating as an unlicensed taxi.

thunderbolt; thunderstone, associated with the orisha Ṣàngó

a dunce, a stupid person, one who receives poor grades
a seller, owner, bearer or maker of mortars

a mud bed in a home used for sitting and sleeping
inner chamber of a room; bedroom

sandstone, a generic name for any type of sedimentary rock
to preach, to give a sermon
A traditional funeral dance and rite performed 8 days after the burial of a person.
A traditional form of oral Yoruba poetry and chants performed by hunters, often praising the orisha Ogun

A form of Yoruba oral praise poetry, (ewì), it is characterized by long epithets praising and acknowledging the traits, accomplishments, and or attributes a specific person, family or lineage, town, animal, òrìṣà, subethnic group etc.
A form of Yoruba oral poetry and chanting performed by the Ekiti people of the Ido-Osi local government in honor of the deity Ọlụ́a.
A form of Yoruba traditional oral poetry and rites performed at the night of the funeral of a great hunter by worshippers of the orisha Ògún.
A form of traditional Yoruba oral poetry characterized by the singing of a bride during marital rites
A genre of oríkì poetry performed by worshippers of the Egúngún consisting of chanting and invocation of the spirit of the Egúngún.
A form of Ekiti music and drum rhythm through the playing of the pípìrì drum.
swallow, specifically the Ethiopian swallow (Hirundo aethiopica)
starling, Poeoptera lugubris, Narrow tailed starling
oxpecker, Buphagus africanus, yellow billed oxpecker

any of a species of red winged turacos, such as the Yellow-billed turaco (Tauraco macrorhynchus
the Yoruba alphabet of the ajami script (as adapted for writing Yoruba).
to practice or engage in Ifa divination
To recite the Qur'an, to recite an Arabic prayer
tilapia, (in particular) Nile tilapia and Oreochromis aureus
precipitation, rain showers, sudden showers of rain

to care for, to have concern for
interpreter, translator, a fluent speaker of a language, bilingual
nickname, alias, a class of Yoruba names normally acquired later in life.
alternative form of pọ́n-ùn (“pound sterling, pound”)
a female virgin, an unmarried woman
a type of yellow or brown non-venomous snake

sacred palm nuts that are used as divination tools in the consulting of Ifa by babalawos. The using of the ikin is reserved for highly important ceremonies (such as during a Àkọsẹ̀jayé) and is how priests determine the signature of an odù Ifá.
A plant of species Megathyrsus maximus
beatboxing, hip-hop music, rap music, pop music
army ant or driver ant, such as those in the genus Dorylus
Any form of pneumonitis or respiratory illness, such as whooping cough, pertussis, bronchitis.

rodents known as anomalures, (specifically) two species of rodent in genus Anomalurus that occur in Nigeria, Beecroft's flying squirrel (Anomalurus beecrofti) and Lord Derby's scaly-tailed squirrel (Anomalurus derbianus), not closely related to the better-known group of flying squirrels of the Sciuridae (squirrel) family
alternative form of ọ̀wọ̀n (“Black-necked spitting cobra”)
powder, flour, sometimes specifically yam flour
Placed before a sentence to make it a yes-no question.
A term of address for royalty and kings, equivalent to the term His Majesty or Her Majesty.
any soft internal organ or body part
a broken calabash, a broken plate, a badly damaged dish no longer suitable for eating.
A bench or seat made from mud and clay attached to the floor of a traditional Ekiti home
Fermented egusi seeds used as a seasoning in soups and stews
niqabi, a woman who practices purdah
a wide shallow pan or pot, used for frying foods like akara
palm frond (in particular) young unwilted palm fronds tied around the body at festivals, such as the Ọlọ́jọ́ festival, by Ògún worshippers.
a rich man, a person with money
female royal court bards or poets who primarily sing the poetry form of ikin and yùngbà in entertainment and praise for the king
the day after tomorrow, next tomorrow
electric eel, fish with electric properties
their alternative form of wọn (“(third-person plural possessive pronoun)”)
your, alternative form of yín (“(second-person plural or honorific possessive pronoun)”)
a bag, or sac, usually containing cowries
alternative form of ẹ̀lùjù (“savanna, forest, wilderness”)
alternative form of àyígbè (“wooden bar used for locking or barricading a door”)
A traditional Yoruba stove or stand often used in outside cooking
a central large inward-facing, typically open roof, hall or courtyard that serves as the main connective and activity space for a dwelling.
the title of the traditional ruler of the town of Àkúrẹ́.
The double-headed axe of the orisha Ṣàngó, often represented in statues and figurines of Ṣàngó, similar to a labrys.
hissing, the act of hissing, the act of sucking one's teeth
god, earth deity or spirit; ancient ancestor (that are worshipped)
someone or something that is neat, clean, fashionable
to repent, to change one's behavior
to put to use; to make important
A supernatural forest or tree creature
pond, lake, pool, any stagnant body of water
any of the various species of wild and cultivated yam in the family Dioscorea
shrike, Lanius gubernator or Lanius mackinnoni
verse, a small section or row of a written text, (in particular) the Bible or Odù Ifá
to flatter, to compliment, to praise
to brew alcohol, to distill alcohol
to mount (as on someone's back), to proceed
to stay aside, to abstain, to distance, to stay away
coronation ceremony for a chief or a king.
patterned group, a homogeneous entity, caravan, series, class
sacred grove where Eégún performers and worshipers meet before a festival or performance
The sacred regalia or costume worn by Egúngún
alternative form of àárọ̀ (“morning, origin, beginning”)
organization, meeting, assembly, the act of doing something together as a group
a praise name or oríkì name for the dove
alternative form of píìrì (“a type of Èkìtì drum”)
upstairs, upper story of a building
at full-length, stretched out, with the whole body
to decide, to resolve, to conclude
the act of disregarding or showing contempt
A type of Yoruba tribal mark or scarification attributed to the Ọ̀yọ́ and other related sub-ethnic groups, such as the and Ìbọ̀lọ́ and Ìbàdàn
to practice or engage in Ifa divination
The plants Nauclea diderrichii and Nauclea latifolia
A drum played during funerals and weddings by the Èkìtì people
The plant Tabernaemontana pachysiphon, known for its healing properties.
The african tulip tree, Spathodea campanulata
A type of alcoholic drink made from corn or millet
palm oil producing square, an arena demarcated for the production of palm oil and its waste products.
A class of deciduous trees usually planted around a house to provide shade, consisting of members of the Moraceae family
alternative form of túḿbá (“to surrender, to apologize”)
co-wife, the other wives of one's husband in a polygamous marriage
A wooden stirring stick used to prepare foods like àmàlà and ẹ̀kọ, similar to a spatula and smaller than the ọmọ odó used for pounding.
in between, in the middle of, midway
in between, in the middle of, midway
makruh, a dishonest act or deed
to reach the age of puberty; to become sexually mature
to sweep, to sweep the ground.
cleaner, sweeper, one who sweeps the ground.
oil; usually in reference to palm kernel oil
parrot feathers, (in particular) the red tail feathers of the grey parrot ("odídẹrẹ́"), often used in traditional rituals and ceremonies.
the act of opening a piece of land for renting as farmland
A divination chain used in the Yoruba religion and Ifá divination, specifically to communicate with the orisha Ọ̀rúnmìlà. It serves a similar purpose to the ikin, but in less formal situations.
salat (The obligatory prayer that Muslims are called to perform five times a day)
fontanelle, soft spot on a baby's head.
A type of traditional Yoruba oral poetry among the Ọ̀yọ́ Yoruba performed by high ranking aristocrat women (wives of chiefs and the king), during festivals to honor the king, the Aláàfin.
A form of Yoruba oral poetry and ceremony performed at Ọ̀yọ́ Yoruba funerals of elderly women. It is usually lead by the head, or alágbaa, of a family worshipping the ọ̀jẹ̀ class of egúngún.
skirt (in particular) one worn under an ìró (“wrapper”); undergarment
A form of traditional Yoruba oral poetry among the Ọ̀yọ́ performed during funeral and burial rites.
A form of oral Yoruba poetry among the Ìjẹ̀ṣà people
nail (may refer to nails of either the toe or finger), claw
A dark powder similar to kohl, made from powered galena or antimony, and used as traditional Yoruba eye makeup
A ritual often performed to produce wealth or money via spiritual means, often characterized by practice of kidnapping and human sacrifice
to perform the duties of a priest of the òrìṣà such as a babalawo or a Mọgbà
tiny bells, often made of brass, put on the surrounding edges of the mother (center) drum, ìyá ìlù in a talking drum ensemble
A type of talking drum in the dùndún ensemble
A species of tree Daniellia oliveri
alternative form of àkíkàràgbá (“broken plate or calabash”)
seven o'clock or nine o'clock p.m.
permission, the act of granting permission, an exemption, waiver
A species of monitor lizard smaller than the Nile monitor; likely the Ornate monitor, known for its very thick skin.
monitor lizard, Varanus niloticus, also translated in modern times as alligator (however alligators are not endemic to Africa)
The tree Crateva religiosa, also known as the sacred garlic pear
A mucilaginous plant similar to ewédú and ọọ́yọ́, used to make a vegetable stew
handle, specifically the handle of a hammer, a sword, a knife, etc.
six pence (sixpence); in modern usage, five kobo
alternative form of ẹ̀gbin (“dirt, filth, refuse”)
a type of thick locally woven fabric often dyed and used to make the innermost layers of clothes like aso oke or the ẹ̀kú of an Egungun
seventeenth day, seventeen days from now
(Àkúrẹ́) extreme joy, enthusiasm, to be ecstatic, to be elated, very happy
to rescue, to save, to deliver
to billow or blow in the wind, to flutter
an amount equal to one farthing (one-quarter of a penny)
kobo, lowest denomination of the Nigerian currency, equal to 1/100 of a naira
threepence; in modern usage, three kobo; pittance
two-hundred cowries; one halfpenny in colonial usage; in modern usage, half a kobo
to do a game, to play

fertile, swampy, irrigated river bank, fadama, paddy

to make an excuse, to give excuses.
to update, to do an update
dehydration, dryness; that which is dry or dried
baby; infant portable in the hand.
one born of the best ideals; person of good ìwà

a white (or black) flywhisk, used as a symbol of authority by chiefs, priests, and monarchs, and also used during dancing and rituals in festivals

alternative form of ị̀gbẹ̀ (“time, occasion, season”)

word play; typically utilizing tone and repetition.
straw mat; usually used for sleeping
the act of observing or watching to ponder

you (emphatic second-person plural honorific personal pronoun)

to kiss one's teeth, to hiss
to camp, to set up an impermanent settlement
to kill people, to commit homicide
grinding stone; (in particular) large grinding stone
upright open-ended log drums with single leather heads fastened and tuned by wooden pegs, sacred to the orisha Ọbàtálá, it is a subfamily of the gbẹ̀du family of drums.
harmattan, harmattan season, cold dry weather
lábàǹkádà cap; a Yorùbá fìlà with two triangular flaps that can be folded, resembling dog's ears.
only, nothing else other than something
a practitioner of the Yoruba religion, Ìṣẹ̀ṣe; a worshipper of the orisha
lightning rod, something that deflects lightning
a respected older male; gentleman, fellow colleague
A beekeeper, someone who sells honey
The African pied hornbill, Lophoceros fasciatus or Tockus fasciatus
The African pied hornbill, Lophoceros fasciatus or Tockus fasciatus
cutlass, machete used by farmers to clear vegetation
alternative form of ìwẹ̀ (“bathing or swimming”)
alternative form of ṣàánú (“to have mercy”)
to be rare, to be special, to be unique
alternative form of ṣọ̀wọ́n (“to be rare”)

A person who makes indigo dye or sells indigo-dyed clothing
The tree Xylopia aethiopica, of which its seeds are used in the spice Grains of Selim
A traditional Yoruba loaded animal suspended trap, consisting of a load of dirt or stone, held by a branch, with food under it.
something that is genuine, authentic, the real deal, valuable
friend, acquaintance, someone who is well-known to a person
to be sweet; to be delicious; to be pleasurable

alternative form of àárọ̀ (“morning, origin, beginning”)
alternative form of àárọ̀ (“morning, origin, beginning”)
alternative form of òwúrọ̀ (“morning, origin, beginning”)
alternative form of òwúrọ̀ (“morning, origin, beginning”)
omele drum; the smallest drum that is highest in pitch, part of bàtá or ẹ̀kù ensembles, beaten with thin sticks made of a flexible raw hide bílálà.

large sonorous type of drum with a skin made from ẹ̀kìrì (black duiker) or ìgalà hide. It is one of the 4 main families of drums among the Yoruba, and is mainly played for the king and by elders including the Ògbóni
The largest drum in any Yoruba ensemble of drums and with the lowest pitch. Each of the 4 families of drums among the Yoruba bàtá, dùndún, sákárà, and gbẹ̀du has the "mother drum."
a male priest, often the chief priest of a specific orisha or deity; see àòrò Ògún
Garcinia kola, The bitter kola nut, it plays an important role in the worship of the òrìṣà, especially that of Ṣàngó
Someone who sells bitter kola nuts (orógbó)
A peanut farmer, someone who sells peanuts
royal courtyard (in particular) the up to 100 courtyards of the palace (àghọ̀fẹn) of Ọ̀ghọ̀, each dedicated to an òrìṣà with its own purpose, as well as the courtyards of the (àghọ̀fẹn) of Oǹdó

primordial spirits in Ìṣẹ̀ṣe, they are the first group of four hundred and one deities (imọlẹ̀) created after the creation of the world and the ones who live in the sky with Olodumare, such as Ògún, Èṣù, Ọ̀rúnmìlà, Ọbàtálá, and Olókun.
A female oríkì name, meaning "One we beg to care for".

An orisha of the forest, including the use of herbs and medicinal charms. She is said to have the head and tail of a dog, a single leg, with the rest of her body being human. She is a close associate of Ọ̀sanyìn.
you (emphatic second-person plural honorific personal pronoun)
The nightingale bird, known in Yoruba proverbs for its beautiful singing
Standard-winged nightjar or Pennant-winged nightjar, traditionally believed to be carry the lightning of the thunder god Ọ̀rámfẹ̀
beads, usually worn as necklaces and bracelets by royalty or traditional priests, ìlẹ̀kẹ̀ often specifically refers to coral beads

In traditional Yoruba society, a symbolic message sent as a form of communication between two entities. The purpose of the àrokò was to send a secret message in which only the two entities communicating would understand what the contents of the àrokò meant.
alternative form of èsúrú (“aerial yam (Dioscorea bulbifera)”)
virgin, a recently married woman who has not had children
to be lukewarm, to be tepid
priests or initiates of the orisha Ọbàtálá

A type of powder from camwood, the wood of the Baphia nitida tree. It is used in the traditional Yoruba religion during divination, in which the powder is sprinkled on the ọpọ́n Ifá, and an odù signature is traced in the powder.
A large metal or tin bucket
the act of dividing something into parts
alternative form of òjíá (“The plant Daniellia ogea, myrrh”)
molue (a type of bus in Lagos)
the fruit of the tree Irvingia gabonensis
woodpecker, specifically the African grey woodpecker
The plant Newbouldia laevis, its leaves are used to adorn newly crowned chiefs
porcupine, specifically African brush-tailed porcupine (Atherurus africanus) or crested porcupine (Hystrix cristata)
porcupine, specifically an African brush-tailed porcupine (Atherurus africanus) or a crested porcupine (Hystrix cristata)
tick, specifically a hard tick of the genus Amblyomma
receipt (written acknowledgement that a sum of money has been received)
A type of toy, it is regarded as a shrine used by children in the worship of the orisha Kórì, (also known as Kórìkóto).
alternative form of kórìkóto (“A toy or shrine used by children”)
rocksalt; potash; any series of minerals like natron often used in cooking
A worshipper or initiate of the orisha Ọ̀ṣun
alternative form of ọ̀páláńbá (“glass, glass bottle”)
alternative form of ọ̀páláńbá (“glass, glass bottle”)
A food eaten by the Ìjẹ̀bú and Rẹ́mọ Yoruba people, made from grated taro (cocoyam), mixed with water, and then boiled. It is normally eaten with a stew like ẹ̀gúsí.
A devotee or worshipper of the orisha Shango
A worshipper or devotee of the orisha Ọ̀sanyìn
A devotee or worshipper of the orisha
A devotee or worshipper of the orisha Ọya
A devotee or initiate of Ifá divination and a worshipper of the orisha Ọ̀rúnmìlà
A female initiate or priestess of Ifa divination
alternative form of àgálámàṣà (“trickery, mischief, dishonesty”)
alternative form of àgálámàṣà (“trickery, mischief, dishonesty”)
a species of hawk or kite
to be big; to be great
alternative form of màjèsín (“child, kid, youth, adolescent”)
A devotee or worshipper of the orisha Èṣù
A Yoruba female hairstyle also known as the pàtẹ́wọ́, featuring braids that swirl across the head.
owner of, or worker in, a fast food restaurant
A type of large garment normally reserved for the wealthy and aristocrats, it denotes high status and power.
to delete, to remove, to throw away
to slip off, to glide off something
A name for a variety of similarly looking trees, including Uapaca heudelotii, Lannea acida, and Lannea welwitschii (also known as orita).
to be intelligent; to know one's stuff
Any two kinds of mushroom which resemble ears (wood ear (Auricularia auricula-judae) and cloud ear fungus (Auricularia cornea))
The edible fungus Schizophyllum commune, which resembles the foot of a chicken
The fungi Coprinellus ephemerus, it is regarded as being poisonous and grows on dunghills
The inedible fungi Daldinia concentrica, it grows on the dead wood of the tree Celtis mildbraedii, where it is said to smell of snuff or tobacco.
to become dry; to become dehydrated
to carve or sculpt; (in particular) to carve or sculpt something out of wood or stone
to toss split kolanuts for divination
to be beautiful; to be handsome
to discuss, to debate, to deliberate
A type of stew or soup made from grounded and cooked beans. When combined with ewédú soup, it forms a stew known as àbùlà
to be stocky; to be built
to perform wudu; to wash parts of the body before prayer
to save; to conserve; to manage (money; energy)
A small machete used to cut grass
A general name for any of the African species of the order Accipitriformes, specifically the hawk, kite, or eagle.
In pre-colonial Yoruba society, the head or ruler of a small village or a town. In modern day Nigeria, most baálẹ̀ have been recognized as a king of their own respective village.
coronet or small crown, worn by chiefs and nobles
ancestor, local earth spirit (imọlẹ̀), the guardian spirit of a specific town or people
to be protruding, large, and heavy
A name for a series of flowering plants
alternative form of aṣọ (“cloth, garment, clothing”)
a term used to refer to people of ethnic groups to the north of Ifẹ̀, including the Nupe people, Hausa people, and Ìyàgbà people.
a type of whistle or horn used by a hunter; dog whistle
alternative form of ọ̀mọ̀wé (“scholar, philosopher, doctor”)
alternative form of ọ̀mọ̀wé (“scholar, philosopher, doctor”)
bush rat; (in particular) Tullberg's soft-furred mouse
alternative form of yẹ̀rì (“skirt worn under an ìró”)
In the Yoruba religion, the concept of the biological, spiritual, and creative powers (or àṣẹ) of all women and mothers
the act of disparaging, disparagement, belittlement
stiltwalker, someone who walks on stilts
A cutting of a mature plant used to cultivate the growing of a new plant, (in particular) a cutting or slice of a yam tuber
A traditional Yoruba cooking broom used as a whisk to mash leaves when making stew. It is mainly used in the preparation of ewédú stew.
A prolonged period of seclusion during which a newly elected Yoruba monarch undergoes ancestral rituals necessary before being formally crowned.
A type of Yoruba incantation or spell, used to ward off a preemptive supernatural attack
ogbono seeds; the seeds from the fruit of the tree Irvingia gabonensis
passage; corridor or hallway in a home or building
The smallest type of Yoruba drum belonging to the dùndún class of Yoruba drums. It is believed to be the oldest type of drum in Yorubaland; and is thus regarded as the ancestor of all other drums.
person from an unprivileged background; a person that hustles
variety; diversity (specified by the following noun)
alternative form of ajẹbọ́tà (“person from a privileged background”)
the act of saying something that takes immediate effect
A Yoruba drum belonging to the gbẹ̀du class of drums, it is used to make coded messages or send long-distance messages. It was mainly played in the royal court of the Aláàfin.
perpendicularity, the state of being perpendicular or vertical
plant, crop, (especially) if it was originally planted
(Ayédé) the tree Landolphia owariensis (white rubbervine, white-ball-rubber), a major source of rubber and latex
A bent stick used to play the Yoruba talking drum (dùndún and gángan families).
A type of drum belonging to the gbẹ̀du family of Yoruba drums, and a subfamily of drums. It is sacred to the orisha Ògún and mainly played by hunters.
Any of several species of plants known for their calming properties, including Kalanchoe pinnata, Emilia fosbergii, Kalanchoe crenata, and Tabernaemontana pachysiphon.
of a ticking sound (for example, of a clock)
to persevere, to try, to make a serious effort

moimoi, a traditional Nigerian steamed bean pudding made from onions, black-eyed beans, red peppers, and other ingredients of choice.
purple yam; Dioscorea alata or Dioscorea smilacifolia
a type of bird in the family Columbidae, noted for being similar to the pigeon

to be picky or fussy about taste or choice of food

a senior practitioner or devotee of an orisha

In Yoruba tradition, a malevolent spirit that causes the premature death of a child, often successive children. It is often addressed through several rituals. (Compare with ogbanje).

profound or deep knowledge or wisdom; complex

any raffia palm tree, including the Raphia vinifera, Raphia farinifera, Raphia hookeri, and Raphia sudanica.

plants pf species Dracaena mannii, Dracaena fragrans, and Dracaena arborea, known primarily for their spiritual and healing properties.
one who stays quiet and doesn't talk

the darkening or reddening of the eyes, usually as a sign of anger

Someone who was never taught manners; a mannerless untrained person.

cow; (in particular) a shorthorn dwarf cow known as muturu. This species of cow is different from the zebu cow known as màlúù

a priest of the worship of the divinity Ọ̀rúnmìlà and Ifa; babalawo

any big river or body of water, usually referring to the Niger river
bend-down select; Okrika; secondhand thrifted clothes, often inexpensive and sold in roadside markets
